evidence_quotes.quote: Legal safeguards against the arbitrary removal or deactivation of purchased digital content
Requirements for companies to allow continued access to single-player or offline modes, even after online services are shut down
Clear and enforceable definitions of digital ownership

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, I would like to strongly urge you to include provisions for the preservation and long-term accessibility of digital video games in the upcoming Digital Fairness Act. As a consumer, I have experienced firsthand the consequences of current digital ownership models. A recent example is the case of The Crew, a game published by Ubisoft. I purchased this game and played it for years, but in 2024, Ubisoft deactivated the game entirely. It was removed from my account, and it is now completely unplayableeven in single-player mode. I paid for this title with the expectation that I would be able to access it as long as I owned the necessary hardware. This practice is deeply unfair to consumers and sets a dangerous precedent for digital media. When a product I legally purchased can be deleted without recourse, it calls into question the very definition of digital ownership. Video games are not only entertainment; they are a significant part of modern culture and history. Preservation should be a priority, both for consumers' rights and for cultural heritage. I respectfully ask that the Digital Fairness Act ensure: Legal safeguards against the arbitrary removal or deactivation of purchased digital content Requirements for companies to allow continued access to single-player or offline modes, even after online services are shut down Clear and enforceable definitions of digital ownership Thank you for considering this important issue. Sincerely
